heb.paragraph: The verb prophēteuō and its cognates dominate ch. 14, appearing over twenty times. In the Pauline usage, prophecy is Spirit-inspired speech that 'strengthens, encourages, and comforts' (v. 3). Unlike tongues (which address God, v. 2), prophecy addresses people in intelligible language. The Corinthians' preference for tongues inverts Paul's priority: intelligible edification trumps impressive but unintelligible spiritual display.

The noun glōssa (tongue, language) appears over twenty times in ch. 14. The debate over whether Pauline glossolalia denotes human languages (as at Pentecost) or ecstatic utterance remains contested. In ch. 14 the emphasis is on unintelligibility: the tongue-speaker utters 'mysteries by the Spirit' (v. 2) and requires an interpreter for the community to benefit (v. 13).

refs.note: Moses' wish that all the Lord's people were prophets is the OT backdrop to Paul's preference for prophecy: Spirit-inspired intelligible speech that builds up the community.
The Pentecost narrative describes glossolalia as xenolalia. Paul's usage in 1 Cor 14 may represent a different or broader phenomenon.

notes.note: Paul opens with a clear hierarchy: 'eagerly desire gifts of the Spirit, especially prophecy.' Prophecy edifies the church directly while tongues edify only the speaker. Tongues without interpretation are private devotion, not public ministry.
The musical analogy is vivid: a flute or harp with indistinct notes communicates nothing; a trumpet with an unclear signal leaves the army unprepared. Paul's criterion is always intelligibility.

The language analogy: the world is full of meaningful languages, but without shared comprehension, speakers are 'foreigners' to each other. Paul redirects Corinthian zeal toward gifts that build up the church.

Calvin observes that Paul does not condemn tongues but subordinates them to prophecy on the principle of edification. Every element of worship must be intelligible to the congregation. A minister who speaks over his people's heads violates the same principle.
The NET note clarifies that 'speaks to God' emphasises the limitation of uninterpreted tongues, not their superiority: only God understands the tongue-speaker.
The NET note on 'greater than' explains that the comparison is functional, not ontological: the prophet is 'greater' in terms of community benefit, not spiritual rank.

Fee, as a Pentecostal scholar, affirms the ongoing validity of tongues while insisting that prophecy is functionally superior in the assembly. Paul redefines 'spirituality' from impressive phenomena to communal edification. Fee notes that Paul values tongues ('I would like every one of you to speak in tongues,' v. 5a) but in their proper sphere.

Thiselton provides sociolinguistic analysis: tongues without interpretation are speech-acts that fail to achieve their perlocutionary effect. Prophecy achieves its purpose because it operates within a shared linguistic framework. The musical analogies draw from Corinth's cultural context—the city had a famous odeum.

hist.context: Chapter 14 applies the principles of ch. 12 (diversity) and ch. 13 (love as criterion) to the specific question of tongues and prophecy. Paul does not prohibit tongues but systematically subordinates them to prophecy on the criterion of community edification (oikodomē). The argument proceeds through analogies: musical instruments (vv. 7–8), foreign languages (vv. 10–11), and the response of outsiders (vv. 23–25).

heb.paragraph: The noun nous (mind) appears four times in vv. 14–19, contrasted with pneuma (spirit). Paul insists on both: 'I will pray with my spirit, but I will also pray with my understanding' (v. 15). The distinction is between ecstatic experience (pneuma) and cognitive comprehension (nous). Paul refuses worship that bypasses the mind.

The noun idiōtēs (outsider) in vv. 16, 23–24 denotes someone not initiated into the practice. The outsider cannot say 'Amen' to an unintelligible thanksgiving (v. 16). Paul's concern is evangelistic: unintelligible worship repels seekers; intelligible prophecy convicts and converts them.

refs.note: God spoke to unbelieving Israel through unintelligible Assyrian speech as judgment. Paul applies this typologically: tongues serve as a sign of judgment against unbelief.
Paul's exhortation to be 'transformed by the renewing of your mind' reflects the same conviction that the mind must be engaged in the Christian life.

notes.note: Tongue-speakers should pray for the ability to interpret. Without interpretation, the mind is 'unfruitful.' The outsider who cannot follow cannot say 'Amen'—a detail about early liturgical practice.
Paul's personal testimony prevents the argument from being read as anti-charismatic. He values tongues but in private devotion. Five intelligible words outweigh ten thousand in a tongue.
Tongues are a sign 'not for believers but for unbelievers'—and specifically a sign of judgment. Prophecy convicts the unbeliever and produces worship: 'God is really among you!' (v. 25).

Calvin draws the foundational Reformed principle that worship must be intelligible. The entire Reformation critique of the Latin Mass finds scriptural warrant here. The Isaiah citation adds a sobering dimension: unintelligible speech in worship may be a sign of divine displeasure.

The NET note on 'pray with my spirit' discusses whether 'spirit' refers to the Holy Spirit, the human spirit, or the spiritual gift. The NET translators favour the human spirit empowered by the Holy Spirit.
The NET note explains Paul's free citation of Isaiah 28:11–12: the 'strange tongues' are the Assyrian language—a sign of judgment on unbelieving Israel.

Fee argues that vv. 14–15 reveal a key distinction: the pneuma can operate independently of the nous in tongues-prayer, but Paul refuses this as the norm for public worship. Paul's affirmation of tongues (v. 18) is genuine Pentecostal experience. The eschatological vision of vv. 24–25 is the goal of corporate worship: a theophany that convicts and converts.

Thiselton provides extensive treatment of the nous/pneuma distinction. Paul's position is neither rationalist nor irrationalist but integrative: authentic worship engages the whole person. The Isaiah citation is typological, not allegorical.

hist.context: Paul deepens the argument by introducing the nous (mind) as indispensable partner to the pneuma (spirit). The rhetorical climax: 'In the church I would rather speak five intelligible words to instruct others than ten thousand words in a tongue' (v. 19). Paul cites Isaiah 28:11–12 to argue that tongues function as a sign of judgment to unbelievers.

heb.paragraph: The adverb euschēmonōs (properly) in v. 40 provides the chapter's concluding principle. The term derives from eu (well) + schēma (form) and denotes behaviour that is both aesthetically appropriate and morally fitting. The contrast is with the akatastasia (disorder, v. 33) that characterised Corinthian worship.

The noun eirēnē (peace) in v. 33—'God is not a God of disorder but of peace'—grounds the call to order in God's character. The Hebrew concept of shalom underlies the Greek: wholeness, harmony, and divine blessing.

refs.note: Paul assumes women pray and prophesy in the assembly, creating tension with 14:34–35. Most scholars conclude that 14:34–35 either addresses a different activity or represents a later interpolation.
Paul's instruction to 'teach and admonish one another through psalms, hymns, and songs from the Spirit' reflects a similar vision of ordered charismatic worship.
Paul balances: 'Do not quench the Spirit. Do not treat prophecies with contempt. But test them all; hold on to what is good.'

notes.note: Paul does not suppress charismatic worship but regulates it. Tongue-speakers are limited to two or three, must speak in turn, and require an interpreter.

Prophets are similarly regulated. The evaluation process (diakrinetōsan) presupposes that prophetic speech must be tested. 'The spirits of prophets are subject to the control of prophets' refutes claims that ecstatic compulsion exempts speakers from order.

The instruction for women to 'remain silent' must be read alongside 11:5. The most likely interpretation is that the silence pertains to the evaluation of prophecy. The closing summary: 'everything should be done in a fitting and orderly way.'

Calvin draws the principle that true freedom and true order are complementary. The Spirit's work creates diversity; love and wisdom create order. On the women's silence passage, Calvin reads it as prohibiting authoritative teaching, consistent with his reading of 1 Tim 2:11–12.

The NET note provides extensive discussion. It notes the Western textual tradition placing vv. 34–35 after v. 40 and suggests the 'silence' may pertain to evaluation of prophecy rather than all forms of speech.
The NET note on 'fitting and orderly' observes that both terms carry connotations of good social order within the Graeco-Roman cultural context.

Fee argues that vv. 34–35 are a non-Pauline interpolation, noting the contradiction with 11:5, the textual fluidity, and the uncharacteristic appeal to 'the law.' Regardless, Fee's reading emphasises that Paul values charismatic worship but insists on order, intelligibility, and mutual edification.

Thiselton reconstructs the Corinthian assembly as a gathering in a private home with 30–50 attendees. On vv. 34–35, he proposes that the 'silence' pertains to disruptive questioning during prophecy evaluation: women should not publicly interrogate their prophesying husbands but 'ask at home.' This preserves both the silence instruction and 11:5's assumption that women prophesy.

hist.context: Paul provides specific regulations: two or three tongue-speakers may speak, one at a time, with interpretation (vv. 27–28); two or three prophets may speak, and others evaluate (vv. 29–30). The controversial instruction about women's silence (vv. 34–35) has generated enormous debate. The textual evidence (vv. 34–35 appear after v. 40 in some Western manuscripts) supports the possibility of interpolation, though the majority retain the verses while debating their scope.

themes.note: Worship and Prophecy share the top score as the chapter is entirely devoted to regulating charismatic worship, with prophecy elevated as the supreme congregational gift.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Thesis: prophecy over tongues on the criterion of edification

rows.label: vv.13–19
rows.text: Mind and spirit: both must be engaged

rows.label: vv.20–25
rows.text: Isaiah citation: tongues as judgment sign; prophecy convicts

rows.label: vv.26–33
rows.text: Worship regulations: tongues and prophecy ordered

rows.label: vv.34–40
rows.text: Women's silence debate; order and propriety

lit.note: Chapter 14 applies the criteria of chs. 12–13 to tongues and prophecy. The governing principle is community edification, requiring intelligibility, order, and love.

hebtext.note: The verb prophēteuō and its cognates dominate ch. 14, appearing over twenty times. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Prophecy and Tongues in Worship

translations.text: Even in the case of lifeless things that make sounds, such as the pipe or harp, how will anyone know what tune is being played unless there is a distinction in the notes?
If even lifeless instruments, such as the flute or the harp, do not give distinct notes, how will anyone know what is played?
Now, brothers and sisters, if I come to you and speak in tongues, what good will I be to you, unless I bring you some revelation or knowledge or prophecy or word of instruction?
Now, brothers, if I come to you speaking in tongues, how will I benefit you unless I bring you some revelation or knowledge or prophecy or teaching?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul opens with a clear hierarchy: 'eagerly desire gifts of the Spirit, especially prophecy.' Prophecy edifies the church directly while tongues edify only the speaker. Tongues without interpretation …"

rec.quote: "Calvin observes that Paul does not condemn tongues but subordinates them to prophecy on the principle of edification. Every element of worship must be intelligible to the congregation. A minister who …"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The NET note clarifies that 'speaks to God' emphasises the limitation of uninterpreted tongues, not their superiority: only God understands the tongue-speaker."

debate: The NET note clarifies that 'speaks to God' emphasises the limitation of uninterpreted tongues, not their superiority: only God understands the tongue…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Prophecy and Tongues in Worship within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
